column1: biography

Bobby Watford, a 40‑year‑old Mexican veterinarian living in Denver, Colorado, grew up on the outskirts of the city and developed a love for animals early on. After graduating from Jefferson High, he earned his DVM at the University of Colorado Boulder, where he also completed a research stint in wildlife health. Fluent in English, Bobby has always described his upbringing as a blend of small‑town curiosity and the vibrant culture of his community, values that continue to shape his compassionate approach to animal care.

Since finishing his training, Bobby has worked at VCA Animal Hospital and later at the Colorado Veterinary Clinic, where he now leads a busy mixed‑practice team. He identifies as a Christian Democrat and often volunteers at local shelters, a habit encouraged by his wife, Maya, and their two teenage children, Aria and Leo. Outside the clinic, Bobby enjoys hiking the Rockies with his family and mentoring veterinary students from his alma mater.

Idilio Bell is a 37‑year‑old astronomer living in Edison, New Jersey. Born on November 21, 1988, he grew up in a bilingual Italian household and speaks English at home and work. He earned his bachelor’s degree in physics from the University of New Jersey and later completed a PhD in astrophysics at Princeton, where his dissertation focused on exoplanet atmospheres. After graduation he spent three years at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center before joining SpaceX’s research division, where he now leads a team analyzing data from the Starlink telescope array. Idilio describes himself as secular and leans progressive on most political issues, often volunteering for science outreach programs in his community.

Outside the lab, Idilio shares a modest house on West Roberts Drive with his wife, Maya, and their two young daughters, Lina and Zara. His mother, Elena, lives nearby and still cooks the family’s favorite pasta on Sundays, while his father, Marco, retired from an engineering firm in New York. Family gatherings are a mix of lively conversation and stargazing sessions on the backyard deck, where Idilio points out constellations and tells stories of the cosmos that inspire his children’s curiosity.

Jodi Allison, 36, lives at 204 Bluegrass in Clayton, North Carolina. A Caucasian editor with a lifelong love of words, she earned her BA in English from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and cut her teeth on the newsroom of the Raleigh Times. After a stint as copy chief at Venture Media, Jodi moved to Southern Publishing, where she now leads the feature‑section team. She describes herself as a moderate Democrat and a Methodist who finds comfort in the rhythm of Sunday worship.  

Outside the office Jodi shares a busy home with her husband, Alex, a school counselor, and their two children, Ethan, 7, and Maya, 4. The family often spends weekends gardening in the yard behind their house or volunteering at the local library’s reading program, a tradition Jodi started as a teenager. Her friends say she balances deadlines with devotion to community, always keeping a notebook handy for the next story that matters.

James Mills is a 69‑year‑old paramedic who lives with his wife, Margaret, in Darien, Illinois. A native of the Midwest with Irish roots, he grew up speaking English and has always felt a strong connection to his community. After earning his EMT certification at the University of Illinois at Chicago, James began his career with American Medical Response, where he spent a decade responding to emergencies across the Chicago suburbs. He later joined Lifeline EMS, a private ambulance service that operates throughout DuPage County, and has been a senior crew member there for the past fifteen years, earning the respect of both his colleagues and the patients he carries home.

Outside of work, James is an active member of his local church and identifies as a Christian. He and Margaret raised three children—two daughters and a son—all of whom have pursued careers in health care or public service. The Mills household often buzzes with visits from their eight grandchildren, who love hearing their grandpa’s stories from night shifts and his quiet advice about staying calm under pressure. Politically, James leans Democrat and volunteers during election season, believing that reliable emergency services are a cornerstone of a caring society.

Nancy Burton is a 21‑year‑old cashier who lives at 297 Tupelo Trail in Ogden, Utah. She was born on May 12, 2004, and grew up in a close‑knit family that includes her parents, Mark and Linda, and her older sister, Jenna. Nancy identifies as White and speaks English at home and work. She was raised in the Christian faith, attending a local Baptist church every Sunday, and she describes her political outlook as moderate, favoring practical community solutions over partisan rhetoric.

Since graduating high school, Nancy has worked as a cashier at a Target store in downtown Ogden and previously spent a summer shift at a Smith’s grocery. She is currently a part‑time student at the University of Utah, majoring in business administration while balancing her schedule between classes and the checkout lane. In her free time she enjoys hiking the nearby Wasatch trails with her family and volunteering at the community food pantry, hoping to give back to the neighborhood that raised her.

Cheryl Gray is a 33‑year‑old historian living at 196 Route 417 in Clarendon Hills, Illinois. A first‑generation Irish American, she grew up reading the local archives with her mother and spent her weekends volunteering at the DuPage County Historical Society. After graduating with honors in History from the University of Chicago, she took a research assistant position at the Smithsonian where she focuses on early 20th‑century urban migration. Cheryl writes her articles in English and contributes regularly to the regional journal Midwest History Review.  

Outside of work, Cheryl shares a small house with her older brother, Michael, and keeps close ties to her parents, Linda and Robert, who encouraged her passion for the past. She describes herself as an atheist and aligns with liberal political ideas, often participating in community forums about historic preservation. In her free time she enjoys documenting oral histories from longtime residents of the Chicago suburbs, hoping to turn her fieldwork into a book that will highlight the everyday lives of ordinary people.

Oscar Valenzano, a 52‑year‑old film director living at 267 Mamalahoa Hwy in San Diego, California, grew up in a Mexican household where English and Spanish were spoken at the kitchen table. He earned his BFA in Film Production from UCLA and later completed an MFA at Columbia, a double dose of West Coast grit and East Coast theory that still informs his visual style. After internships at Paramount and a junior edit position at Netflix, Oscar broke through with his first feature, a low‑budget indie that earned a spot at Sundance and secured a distribution deal with A24. His early work is marked by a quiet fascination with ordinary people facing moral dilemmas, a theme he traces back to the stories his grandfather told him on long drives along the Pacific.

Today Oscar splits his time between directing commercials for Apple and shepherding the scripts of up‑and‑coming writers at his own boutique company, Harbor Light Pictures. He identifies as Catholic and votes progressive, often speaking at local panels about the need for diversity behind the camera. At home he shares his love of cinema with his wife Maya, a documentary producer, and their two teenage children, Lucia and Diego, who are already tinkering with a family DSLR and dreaming of their own shortcuts to the silver screen.

Darwin Santalucia is a 33‑year‑old carpenter living at 13 NE Aztec Blvd in Orem, Utah. A proud Mexican who grew up speaking Spanish at home, he attended the University of Utah where he earned an associate’s degree in construction technology before joining the trade. After apprenticeships with LDS Builders and Mountain Timber, Darwin started his own shop, Santalucia Woodworks, in 2019, specializing in custom furniture and sustainable home renovations for families across Utah County. He identifies as Mormon and describes himself as fiscally conservative, values he says were instilled by his father, John, a retired electrician, and his mother, Maria, who still runs a small bakery downtown.

Outside the workshop, Darwin is a husband to Sara, his college sweetheart, and the father of two young children, Maya and Leo. Weekends often find him coaching his son’s soccer team or helping his sister, Elena, with her landscaping business. Friends know him for his quiet humor and his habit of ending each project with a handwritten note in English thanking the client for the opportunity. He remains active in the local church’s community service program, building playgrounds and repairing homes for low‑income families, a commitment he says reflects both his faith and his belief in giving back to the community that raised him.

Richard Hasty, 43, has spent most of his adult life among the stacks of the Kennebunkport Public Library, where he now serves as senior librarian. A native of York County, he grew up speaking English and identifies as Irish. After earning a BA in History from the University of Maine, he completed his MLS at Simmons College, and his early career saw him cataloguing collections at the Portland Regional Library and later at the Bowdoin College Library before returning home in 2012. His keen eye for organization and a love of local history helped him launch the town’s digital archives project, a partnership that involved working with the state historic preservation office and the nonprofit group Harbor Books.

Off the clock, Richard lives at 500 E Purdue Dr with his wife, Maya, and their two teenage children, Lily and Jonah. A practicing Catholic, he attends Mass on Sundays and volunteers as a literacy tutor for adult learners. Politically, he describes himself as moderate and often contributes to town council discussions about public funding for cultural programs. In his spare time he enjoys sailing on Casco Bay, collecting first‑edition novels, and organizing the annual summer reading festival that brings together families from across York County.

Yanely Rodriguez is a 25‑year‑old construction worker living at 481 SW 134 St. in Long Beach, California. born in 2001, she grew up in a Mexican family that taught her the value of hard work and community. After graduating from Long Beach City College with an associate’s degree in civil engineering technology, Yanely started her career on the job site of Gulf Coast Construction, where she learned the basics of framing and concrete finishing. She later moved to Pacific Builders, a regional contractor known for its mixed‑use projects, and now helps lead the crew that renovates apartments along the waterfront. Yanely speaks English at home and at work, and she attends mass on Sundays, identifying as Catholic.

Outside the site, Yanely is close to her parents, Carlos and Maya, and her younger brother, Luis, who is finishing high school. She is a moderate Democrat who supports affordable housing initiatives and labor rights, often volunteering with local unions to mentor new workers. In her free time she loves surfing at nearby beaches, cooking traditional dishes for family gatherings, and listening to old rock records while she plans her next career certification. Her friends describe her as reliable, friendly, and always ready to lend a hand.

Bradley Kelly is a 39‑year‑old economist who lives with his wife Maya and their two children in Durham, North Carolina. He was born on October 29, 1986, and identifies as Irish; English is his native language. Bradley works as a senior economist at a regional research institute that advises local governments on fiscal policy, and he often contributes op‑eds to the state newspaper. He remains close to his parents, who still live in the same county, and he enjoys weekend bike rides with his sister Jenna.

Bradley earned his bachelor’s degree in economics from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and later completed a Ph.D. in economics at Stanford. After graduating he spent several years at the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond before moving to Deloitte, where he led a team on macro‑economic forecasting. In 2022 he joined his current employer, bringing his expertise in labor market dynamics to public policy projects. Politically, he describes himself as a Democrat and is agnostic in matters of faith. In his free time he volunteers at a local food bank and follows a passion for sailing on the nearby lakes.

David Harrell, a 35‑year‑old electrician, was born on August 9, 1990, and grew up in Lincoln, Nebraska. He is Caucasian and speaks English at home, where he lives with his wife Sarah and their two young children, Mia and Ethan. After graduating from the University of Nebraska–Lincoln with a degree in electrical engineering technology, David married Sarah, a nurse, and they settled in a modest house on Breton Blvd. He attends a local Baptist church on Sundays and describes his political stance as moderate, often voicing support for practical community projects.

Professionally, David began his career with Lincoln Electric, where he learned the trade fundamentals before moving to Midwest Power Services, a contractor that handles commercial wiring and renewable‑energy installations across the region. Over the past decade he has earned certifications in high‑voltage systems and solar panel integration, allowing him to lead crews on large‑scale projects for schools and municipal buildings. Colleagues know him for his calm problem‑solving style and his willingness to mentor apprentices, a habit he attributes to the values instilled by his own mentors and his family’s emphasis on hard work.

Kyndra Dick is a 42‑year‑old data analyst living in Honeoye Falls, New York. Born on December 12, 1983, she grew up in a close‑knit African family that valued education and hard work, and she still speaks English at home and with friends. After graduating from the University of Rochester with a degree in statistics, Kyndra began her career at Blue Ridge Analytics, where she helped build predictive models for regional retailers. She later moved to Nexis Corp, a fintech startup in Rochester, before joining the analytics team at the New York State Department of Health, where she now turns complex health data into actionable insights for policymakers.

Outside of work, Kyndra is a devoted Christian who describes herself as politically moderate, often attending community town halls to stay informed. She lives with her husband, Michael, and their two teenage children, Maya and Aaron, in a modest house on 14th St W. In her free time she enjoys hiking in the Finger Lakes, cooking family recipes, and volunteering at a local library’s adult‑literacy program.

Shane Wile, 61, lives at 7 Mounds Rd in Coopersburg, Pennsylvania. An Irish American who speaks English, he has built a career as a statistician after earning a B.S. in mathematics from the University of Pennsylvania and a Ph.D. in statistics from Penn State. Early on he sharpened his analytical skills at Pfizer and later at IBM before joining Meridian Data Solutions, where he now leads a team developing predictive models for health care clients. His work has taken him to conferences in Chicago and London, and he is known for translating complex data into clear recommendations for decision makers.  

Outside the office, Shane has been married to Linda for 35 years and is the proud father of Emily, a teacher, and Michael, an engineer. The couple enjoys hosting family gatherings that often include their three grandchildren, and they actively participate in their local Christian church. Politically, Shane describes himself as moderate and volunteers with a bipartisan civic group that promotes data‑driven public policy. He spends his free time hiking the nearby Appalachian Trail and tending to a small vegetable garden behind his home.

Anne Johnson is a 52‑year‑old historian who lives at 165 United States Highway 701 South in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. A first‑generation German American, she grew up speaking English at home and developed a love of local archives while helping her mother catalog family photographs. Anne earned her bachelor’s degree in History from the University of Wisconsin–Madison and later completed a PhD at Harvard, focusing on Midwestern labor movements. She met her husband, Mark, during a summer research fellowship, and the couple now raise two teenagers who share their curiosity about the past.

Today Anne works as a senior researcher for the Wisconsin Historical Society, where she curates exhibitions and writes articles on the state’s industrial heritage. She has also consulted for the Smithsonian and contributed to documentary projects for public television. A practicing Christian and a registered Democrat, Anne often volunteers at her church’s food pantry and participates in local civic forums. She remains close to her parents, who still live nearby, and enjoys weekend trips with her family to historic sites across the Midwest.

Aiyanna Redbird is a 57‑year‑old firefighter who lives with her husband Tom at 68 Bliss Cir in the quiet town of Fosters, Greene County, Alabama.  Born on November 30, 1968, she grew up steeped in her Cherokee heritage and attends a local Protestant church, where the community’s songs and stories still echo in her heart.  Aiyanna proudly identifies as a Republican and often shares meals and stories with her two adult children, Michael and Lila, who visit on holidays and help her tend the garden that borders the house.

After graduating from the University of Alabama with a degree in fire science, Aiyanna began her career in 1986 with the Greene County Fire Department, later gaining experience at Alabama Fire Protection Services before returning to her hometown station as a senior firefighter.  Her days are filled with fire calls, rescue trainings, and mentoring new recruits, and she frequently volunteers with the Cherokee Cultural Center to teach fire safety to children.  Outside the firehouse, Aiyanna enjoys woodworking, listening to tribal drums, and staying active in local civic meetings.

Somchai Vongpradit is a 69‑year‑old senior editor who splits his time between his home at 89 Prairie Ln in Flushing, Queens, and the bustling newsrooms of New York City. After earning a BA in English from the University of California, Los Angeles and an MA in Publishing from Columbia University, he began his career as a copy editor at The New York Times before moving on to a long stint at Penguin Random House, where he helped shape dozens of nonfiction titles. He now leads the editorial team at a boutique publishing house called East River Press, guiding new voices and overseeing the final polish of manuscripts that range from memoirs to investigative journalism.

Outside the office, Somchai is a devoted Buddhist and an active member of his local temple, where he volunteers to teach writing workshops to youth. A lifelong Republican, he frequently writes op‑eds on media ethics and free speech. He and his wife, Ladda, have raised three children—two daughters and a son—who live throughout the United States, and they enjoy regular visits from six grandchildren who love hearing stories about his early days in the industry.

Jade Williams, a 24‑year‑old attorney, grew up at 73 North Dent Avenue in Bowling Green, Ohio. A proud Black Caribbean descendant, she earned her bachelor's degree in political science from the University of Kentucky before completing her Juris Doctor at the same school’s College of Law. After passing the Ohio Bar, Jade spent a summer clerkship with the public defender’s office in Wood County and then joined the boutique firm Smith & Partners, where she now focuses on civil rights and housing litigation. She identifies as Protestant and is an active Democrat, regularly volunteering for local campaigns and community voter‑registration drives.

Jade’s family is a central part of her life; she lives with her mother, Marsha, who instilled in her a strong sense of justice, and she often helps her younger brother, Malik, with his schoolwork. When she isn’t in the courtroom, she enjoys listening to reggae, cooking traditional Caribbean dishes for her friends, and hiking the nearby state parks. Her goal is to one day open her own practice that provides affordable legal services to underserved neighborhoods throughout Ohio.

Reza Amir is a 65‑year‑old carpenter who lives at 53 County Road N5578 in Naples, Collier County, Florida. An Iranian American, he grew up hearing stories of his grandparents’ craft in Tehran and turned that heritage into a lifelong love of woodwork. After earning an associate’s degree in construction technology from the University of Florida, Reza began his career with Gulf Coast Builders, later moving to Coastal Custom Woodworks where he helped design and restore dozens of homes along the Gulf. He now runs his own shop, Everglades Carpentry, where he specializes in handmade furniture and historic renovation projects.

Reza is a Muslim and a progressive who votes for candidates who support workers’ rights and environmental stewardship. He shares his home with his wife, Laleh, and their two adult children, Sara and Kian, who occasionally join him in the workshop on weekends. When he isn’t shaping oak or pine, Reza enjoys fishing on the Everglades and volunteering at the local community garden, where he mentors younger craftsmen and promotes sustainable building practices.

Mekonnen Alem is a 66‑year‑old genderqueer teacher who lives at 152 Caseys Ranch Road in the small town of Freetown, Indiana. Proud of their Ethiopian heritage, they have spent the past two decades shaping adult learners at the Brown County Community College, where they teach history and civics. Their classroom is known for lively discussions about local politics, and they openly identify as a Democrat while attending an Eastern Orthodox congregation on Sundays.

Mekonnen earned a bachelor’s degree in History from the University of Michigan and later completed a master’s in Education at Indiana University Bloomington. Before returning to academia, they worked for the Indiana Department of Education and helped develop curriculum for the Freetown Literacy Project, a nonprofit focused on early‑grade reading. At home, Mekonnen shares a quiet home with their partner, Lule, and their grown‑up children, Hana and Kofi, who often visit on holidays to celebrate family traditions rooted in their Ethiopian background.

Anita Patel, 57, is an Indian American real estate agent who calls 248 Beach Lane in La Mesa, California her home. After earning a Bachelor of Arts in Business from San Diego State University, she began her career with Coldwell Banker before joining Keller Williams, where she now helps buyers and sellers navigate the San Diego County market with a focus on family‑friendly neighborhoods. Her deep knowledge of the area and steady work ethic have earned her a reputation for honesty and detail, and she often credits her upbringing in a close‑knit Indian American community for shaping her client‑first approach.

Anita is a devout Hindu and a proud Republican, values she shares with her husband Raj, a retired engineer, and their two adult children, Maya and Arjun. The couple enjoys weekends at the beach and frequently hosts cultural gatherings that blend traditional Indian cuisine with Southern California flavors. Their three grandchildren keep them busy, and Anita often volunteers at the local temple’s youth program, believing that strong family ties and community service are the cornerstones of a fulfilling life.

Isabel Martínez is a 45‑year‑old Colombian translator living at 157 Newell Rd in Lenoir, North Carolina. Born on January 24 1981, she grew up in a close‑knit family with her mother, Carmen, and her younger brother, Diego, who still visits often. After graduating from the University of North Carolina at Charlotte with a degree in Spanish‑English linguistics, Isabel began working as a freelance translator before earning a master’s certificate in technical translation from the American Translators Association. She is Catholic and identifies as a conservative, values she often discusses with friends at her local church.

Professionally, Isabel has spent the past twenty‑three years translating legal, medical, and marketing documents for firms such as TransLingua Solutions, Global Voice Inc., and the multinational publisher HarperCollins. She now leads a small team at a boutique agency in Raleigh, where she mentors junior translators and manages projects for South American clients. Outside of work, Isabel enjoys hiking in the Appalachian foothills, cooking traditional Colombian dishes for her partner, Carlos, and volunteering as a language tutor for recent immigrants in Caldwell County.

Mildred Johnson is an 62‑year‑old bartender who has called Tucson, Arizona home for most of her life. She works the night shift at the Sun Dial Bar on 5th Avenue, a cozy spot known for its desert‑inspired cocktails, and she previously poured drinks at the Elixir Lounge and the Desert Rose Bar. Mildred earned an associate’s degree in hospitality from the University of Arizona, and her steady hand behind the bar reflects the training she received there. A practicing Protestant, she enjoys Sunday services at a nearby Baptist church and describes herself as a moderate when it comes to politics.

Mildred lives at 174 Southside Dr with a garden of marigolds that she tends when she’s off duty. She was married to her high school sweetheart, James, who passed away three years ago, and she raises her two children, Carl and Denise, who live nearby with their families. Her days are often filled with visits from grandchildren who love to watch her shake a shaker and share stories from Tucson’s past. In her spare time she volunteers at a community center, plays the piano for senior gatherings, and still swears by the peach iced tea she first learned to make for her mother.

Atsa Yazzie was born on June 3 1953 and grew up on the Navajo reservation before her family moved to the Central Valley. After graduating from the University of California, Davis with a degree in nursing, she began working at Sutter Medical Center in nearby Yuba City and later at Trinity Health in Modesto, where she spent most of her career caring for patients in the San Joaquin County area. Atsa’s dedication to her patients earned her a reputation for calm, thorough care, and she remained a full‑time nurse well into her sixties, eventually transitioning to a part‑time role at a community clinic close to her home on 189 Slash Pine Ct, Escalon, CA 95320.

Now retired, Atsa lives with her husband John and enjoys gardening with their grandchildren, Michael and Lily. She identifies as Navajo, follows the Catholic faith, and holds libertarian views, often volunteering on local civic projects that promote individual freedom. The close‑knit family gathers regularly for holiday meals, sharing stories of her long nursing career and the values she passed down to the younger generations.

Jinhee Park, 34, is a Korean American graphic designer living at 745 North Tamarisk Street in Corona, California. She earned her BFA in Visual Communications from the University of California, Irvine, where she first fell in love with motion graphics and branding. After graduating, Jinhee joined PixelWave Studios, a boutique agency in Los Angeles, and later moved to BrightSide Creative in San Diego, where she led several award‑winning campaigns for tech startups. In 2020 she accepted a senior role at Lumen Design Agency, overseeing a cross‑functional team that creates visual identities for nonprofit organizations across the West Coast.  

Outside of work Jinhee is a Protestant who volunteers at her local community center, teaching basic design skills to under‑served youth. A self‑identified Progressive, she frequently contributes op‑eds on inclusive design practices to regional publications. She shares her home with her younger brother, Min‑jun, and often visits her parents, who immigrated from Seoul, for family gatherings centered around Korean food and shared stories.
